answer: You can do anything you want with the source, as long as you leave the copyrights in and display those copyrights in any documentation about Python that you produce. If you honor the copyright rules, it’s OK to use Python for commercial use, to sell copies of Python in source or binary form (modified or unmodified), or to sell products that incorporate Python in some form.

Python versions are numbered “A.B.C” or “A.B”: A is the major version number, B is the minor version number, and C is the micro version number. Feature releases increment the minor version number, bugfix releases increment the micro version number, and development releases are denoted as alpha, beta, or release candidate with suffixes “aN”, “bN”, or “rcN”. The order of precedence is: alpha versions precede beta versions, which precede release candidate versions, which precede the final release.

Feature releases introduce new features and are issued every 12 months as of version 3.9. Bugfix releases are managed for stability, include only fixes for known problems, guarantee that interfaces remain the same throughout a series, and are issued for older versions as needed.

Python has a very simple and consistent syntax and a large standard library, allowing students to concentrate on important programming skills such as problem decomposition and data type design. Students can be quickly introduced to basic concepts such as loops and procedures and can work with user-defined objects in their first course. The standard library enables students to be assigned programming projects early in the course that do something realistic, teaching code reuse and allowing them to gain satisfaction from practical applications. The interactive interpreter helps students test language features and access documentation easily while programming.

Providing a gradual upgrade path is necessary if a feature has to be changed. PEP 5 describes the procedure followed for introducing backward-incompatible changes while minimizing disruption for users.
